source:
Speaker: OK. Ohh, OK.
Speaker: Hello?
Speaker: Hello, can you hear me?
Speaker: Hello. Uh, just before we go any further, can I confirm your name and, age please?
Speaker: Uh, my name's Itha, and I'm twenty six.
Speaker: Twenty six, OK. And how can I help you this afternoon?
Speaker: Um, so basically, I've been really stressed at work for the past few days.
Speaker: And, I've realised that I've got, really dry itchy skin.
Speaker: OK. Um, sorry to hear work's been so stressful. Um.
Speaker: Tell me more about your skin first, Lou. Tell me uh, you mentioned it's been dry and itchy. Um, whereabouts on your skin?
Speaker: All over my arms, um, and my hands mainly.
Speaker: OK. Um, and is this something you've had before?
Speaker: Uh, no. Never had .
Speaker: So, uh, did it, did it come about quite suddenly, or was it more, kind of a gradual thing?
Speaker: I think it started a few days ago.
Speaker: OK. And, has this spread anywhere else? Has it spread to your, to your chest, your legs, your face?
Speaker: No, OK. So apart from being dry and itchy, um, any other symptoms? Has it been sore? Has it been, uh, have you noticed any cracked skin?
Speaker: Um, so my skin's quite red, um, and I'm using coconut butter for the past few days.
Speaker: OK. Has that.
Speaker: I don't have any spots, and no fever , and I'm not sweating either.
Speaker: OK.
Speaker: Thank you, for mentioning all those. Um, OK. Uh, so the coconut butter hasn't helped, with the itch?
Speaker: No, .
Speaker: OK. And, uh, you mentioned work's been quite stressful. What do you do for work?
Speaker: Um, I work as a teacher.
Speaker: OK. And, uh, in what way has work been stressful?
Speaker: I think the kids have been really annoying these days.
Speaker: OK. .
Speaker: And, I've been working really long hours.
Speaker: OK, all right. Well I'm sorry to hear that, and I hope it gets better.
Speaker: Um, going back to your skin. I mean is it anything you've done perhaps, uh, which may have triggered your symptoms? Anything you've touched or, you know, for example shower gels, soaps, fabric , fabric cleaners?
Speaker: , I went swimming a few days ago.
Speaker: OK. Is that the first time in your life or have you been before?
Speaker: Uh, no, I've been before.
Speaker: OK. And did your symptoms start after you went swimming?
Speaker: Uh, yes, .
Speaker: OK, all right. Um, you mentioned you've had no other fevers, is that right? No fevers? No vomiting?
Speaker: No.
Speaker: No. How's your health otherwise?
Speaker: It's pretty good, yeah. I'm not on any medication.
Speaker: And all , we'll get to medication in just a second. Um, , do you, do you feel generally run-down? Do you feel tired?
Speaker: Uh, yeah, I think I have been, quite tired.
Speaker: OK, OK. Are you eating and drinking OK?
Speaker: Yeah, I've been drinking, and eating as normal.
Speaker: Your appetite's fine is it? OK. And your bowels, are your bowels working OK?
Speaker: Yeah.
Speaker: Uh, no, no problems with vomiting, or loose stools?
Speaker: No.
Speaker: OK. And your waterworks are uh, all OK? You're passing urine OK?
Speaker: Yep.
Speaker: OK, all right. Um, so just moving on. In terms of your other medical history, is there anything else I should be aware of?
Speaker: I'm currently taking .
Speaker: Microgynon.
Speaker: Yeah.
Speaker: OK. Do you know what that is?
Speaker: Uh, no.
Speaker: OK. , it's a contraceptive pill
Speaker: Does that make sense? Is that something you're, is that, does that ring a bell now?
Speaker: Ohh yeah.
Speaker: OK. Is there, any other medications that you take?
Speaker: Uh, no.
Speaker: No. So, in terms of your medical history, there's no, problems of skin problems in the past, things like eczema or, um, dermatitis?
Speaker: Uh, no, I think it might just be the weather.
Speaker: Sorry what was that? You think it's.
Speaker: The .
Speaker: I think it might just be the weather.
Speaker: The weather?
Speaker: Yeah, .
Speaker: Ohh, you think the , OK. You think the weather, OK.
Speaker: OK. No that's OK, no, no problems. Well um, uh, move . Do you, do you suffer from allergies? Are you someone who suffers from allergies?
Speaker: Um.
Speaker: Food allergies? Medication allergies?
Speaker: I think, um, I might be lactose intolerant.
Speaker: Lactose intolerant, OK, that could be helpful to know. Um, fine. And tell me of family history, is there anything in the family that you're aware of?
Speaker: Um, yeah, so um, my dad has asthma, and, my mum has hayfever.
Speaker: Hayfever, OK. Um, fine, but any, again, any skin problems that you're aware of?
Speaker: Yep.
Speaker: Uh, no.
Speaker: No, OK, all right. Socially, who do you live with at home?
Speaker: Ohh, it's just me.
Speaker: Live alone?
Speaker: Yep.
Speaker: And, uh, do you smoke at all? Do you, can I ask? Do you smoke? Are you a smoker?
Speaker: Uh, no, I don't smoke.
Speaker: Do you drink much in the way of alcohol?
Speaker: Uh, no.
Speaker: Recreational drugs?
Speaker: No.
Speaker: OK. And you said you , you work as a primary school teacher?
Speaker: Yes, that's right.
Speaker: Uh , the reason I ask is sometimes stress can be a , contributing factor. Um, you felt, you noticed yourself, find, uh, , life's been more stressful recently. Is that right?
Speaker: Do you think that might have any correlation to your symptoms?
Speaker: I don't. I certainly think so.
Speaker: OK, all right. Um, fine. Um.
Speaker: OK, well, normally at this stage I like to proceed to examination, but um, based on your uh, story.
Speaker: Um, it's difficult. I think that, you know, , you've, essentially the story is you've got a dry itchy rash on your, mainly your hands. you mentioned. Um and your arms which started, perhaps after swimming a few days ago.
Speaker: Um, but no other symptoms, you're feeling otherwise, well, no fevers, no bleeding or discharge from skin.
Speaker: Yep.
Speaker: I wonder whether you may have something called dermatitis. Um, have you heard of that condition before?
Speaker: I have, yeah.
Speaker: OK. Um, great. Well I can give you a patient information leaflet about that, which gives a bit more information about the disease, but, um, it, it's something that we can readily treated. Um.
Speaker: With couple options, we have steroid creams I can give you, um, which will help with the inflammation.
Speaker: And, uh, something called emollients, which can help with the dry, dry skin.
Speaker: And that's something you just need to kind of paste on, uh, on to your skin, uh, , throughout the day for the next few days. Um, and hopefully that helps your symptoms.
Speaker: OK, it's cool. Thank you very much.
Speaker: Um, , you can also try antihistamines, so you can get things over the counter, Piriton, Loratadine, they can sometimes help with the itchiness.
Speaker: Yeah, that sounds good.
Speaker: OK. And um, that's probably what I recommend. Paracetamol on this occasion, wouldn't be helpful.
Speaker: Um, uh, uh, painkillers wouldn't be helpful , on this occasion, but let's, let's, let's see how the next few days go.
Speaker: And if it's still not getting better, come back and see me next week, and we can have another look again, at your symptoms.
Speaker: OK, perfect. Thank you very much.
Speaker: Right. Do you have any questions for me?
Speaker: Uh, no.
Speaker: OK. Well, I wish you all the best. Have a good day.
Speaker: Well, thank you very much. Bye.
Speaker: Thank you. Bye bye.
reference:
3/7 hx of dry itchy skin, mainly on the hands and palms. No spread elsewhere. No bleeding/discharge. No cracked skin. Sx started after going swimming. No prev skin problems. 
No fevers. No vomiting. 
Appetite maintained, nil nausea/vomiting 
Nil bowel/urinary sx 

PMH: Lactose intolerance
DH: microgynon
NKDA
FH:  Father - asthma, Mother - hayfever 
SH: - lives alone, nil smoker/etOH/recreational drugs , works as primary school teacher. Recent stress ++

Imp: dermatitis

Plan:
1.	Steroid cream BD
2.	Emollients - regular use QDS
3.	Antihistamines 
4.	Review sx in 1/52, or earlier if worsening sx/any concerns
